text: Good afternoon. Thank you for the opportunity to present our work. The authors have nothing to disclose.
This is an interesting case of a twenty-six-year-old male with no past medical or surgical history who initially presented with a two-day history of nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and
The CT scan image shows a duodenal obstruction, with red arrows indicating the dilation of the proximal duodenum and stomach.
The blue arrow indicates the cecum located in the right upper quadrant of the abdomen, with contrast esophagram findings suggesting intestinal malrotation.
The video shows the abnormal position of the ligament of Treitz, with the duodenum failing to cross the midline and extending caudally on the right side of the abdominal cavity.
The diagnosis of intestinal obstruction secondary to intestinal malrotation was made, and the patient was taken to the operating room for a laparoscopic LAD procedure.
Upon entering the abdominal cavity, the surgeon observes a dilated stomach and duodenum, along with a high-riding cecum located in the right upper quadrant.
The surgeon lyses adhesions between the right colon and the lateral abdominal wall using a combination of countertraction and thermal energy, visibly separating the adhered tissues.
The surgeon reflects the colon medially to expose the duodenum and pylorus, revealing their anatomical positions within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon mobilizes the duodenum and performs a partial Kocher maneuver. The duodenum is observed to be positioned on the right side of the abdominal cavity, lacking its normal C-shape.
The surgeon uses sharp dissection and a harmonic scalpel to lyse adhesions between the colon and duodenum, carefully separating these structures.
Ladd bands are visible in the right upper quadrant, adhering the base of the cecum to the abdominal wall.
The surgeon runs the small bowel distally, systematically freeing adhesions encountered along its length using surgical instruments.
The surgeon encounters a thick fibrous band between the small bowel and mesentery, which is carefully dissected using sharp instruments.

The surgeon identifies a thick fibrous band as the culprit adhesion causing obstruction. After performing adhesiolysis, the duodenum and jejunum are visibly unobstructed, allowing the mesentery to broaden.

The surgeon performs an appendectomy, ligating the mesoappendix with a harmonic scalpel. The appendix is removed using two endo loops, and the appendiceal stump is cauterized and secured with a PDS suture.
The surgeon places the cecum in the left lower quadrant of the abdominal cavity while ensuring the duodenum remains positioned on the right side.

Postoperatively, the patient did well. The nasogastric tube was removed on postoperative day two and was started on a clear liquid diet. On postoperative day three, the patient was advanced to regular diet without any issues and was discharged the following day and on postoperative day four without any complications. At two months follow-up, the patient continued to do well without any issues.
